CSE347Analysis of Algorithms (Lecture 9)

CSE347 Analysis of Algorithms (Lecture 9)

Randomized Algorithms

Hashing

Hashing with chaining:

Input: We have integers in range [1,n−1]=U[1,n-1]=U[1,n−1]=U. We want to map them to a hash table TTT with mmm slots.

Hash function: h:U→[m]h:U\rightarrow [m]h:U→[m]

Goal: Hashing a set S⊆US\subseteq US⊆U, ∣S∣=n|S|=n∣S∣=n into TTT such that the number of elements in each slot is at most 111.

Collisions

When multiple keys are mapped to the same slot, we call it a collision, we keep a linked list of all the keys that map to the same slot.

Runtime of insert, query, delete of elements =Θ(length of the chain)=\Theta(\textup{length of the chain})=Θ(length of the chain)

Worst-case runtime of insert, query, delete of elements =Θ(n)=\Theta(n)=Θ(n)

Therefore, we want chains to be short, or Θ(1)\Theta(1)Θ(1), as long as ∣S∣|S|∣S∣ is reasonably sized, or equivalently, we want the number in any set SSS to hash uniformly across all slots.

Simple Uniform Hashing Assumptions

The nnn elements we want to hash (the set SSS) is picked uniformly at random from UUU. Therefore, we could see that this simple hash function works fine:

h(x)=xmod  mh(x)=x\mod mh(x)=xmodm

Question: What happens if an adversary knows this function and designs SSS to make the worst-case runtime happen?

Answer: The adversary can make the runtime of each operation Θ(n)\Theta(n)Θ(n) by simply making all the elements hash to the same slot.

Randomization to the rescue

We don’t want the adversary to know the hash function based on just looking at the code.

Ideas: Randomize the choice of the hash function.

Randomized Algorithm

Definition

A randomized algorithm is an algorithm the algorithm makes internal random choices.

2 kinds of randomized algorithms:

  1. Las Vegas: The runtime is random, but the output is always correct.
  2. Monte Carlo: The runtime is fixed, but the output is sometimes incorrect.

We will focus on Las Vegas algorithms in this course.

O(n)=E[T(n)]O(n)=E[T(n)]O(n)=E[T(n)] or some other probabilistic quantity.

Randomization can help

Ideas: Randomize the choice of hash function hhh from a family of hash functions, HHH.

If we randomly pick a hash function from this family, then the probability that the hash function is bad on any particular set SSS is small.

Intuitively, the adversary can not pick a bad input since most hash functions are good for any particular input SSS.

Universal Hashing: Goal

We want to design a universal family of hash functions, HHH, such that the probability that the hash table behaves badly on any input SSS is small.

Universal Hashing: Definition

Suppose we have mmm buckets in the hash table. We also have 222 inputs x≠yx\neq yx=y and x,y∈Ux,y\in Ux,y∈U. We want xxx and yyy to be unlikely to hash to the same bucket.

HHH is a universal family of hash functions if for any two elements x≠yx\neq yx=y,

Prh∈H[h(x)=h(y)]=1mPr_{h\in H}[h(x)=h(y)]=\frac{1}{m}Prh∈H​[h(x)=h(y)]=m1​

where hhh is picked uniformly at random from the family HHH.

Universal Hashing: Analysis

Claim: If we choose hhh randomly from a universal family of hash functions, HHH, then the hash table will exhibit good behavior on any set SSS of size nnn with high probability.

Question: What are some good properties and what does it mean by with high probability?

Claim: Given a universal family of hash functions, HHH, S={a1,a2,⋯ ,an}⊂NS=\{a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n\}\subset \mathbb{N}S={a1​,a2​,⋯,an​}⊂N. For any probability 0≤δ≤10\leq \delta\leq 10≤δ≤1, if n≤2mδn\leq \sqrt{2m\delta}n≤2mδ​, the chance that no two keys hash to the same slot is ≥1−δ\geq1-\delta≥1−δ.

Example: If we pick δ=12\delta=\frac{1}{2}δ=21​. As long as n<2mn<\sqrt{2m}n<2m​, the chance that no two keys hash to the same slot is ≥12\geq\frac{1}{2}≥21​.

If we pick δ=13\delta=\frac{1}{3}δ=31​. As long as n<43mn<\sqrt{\frac{4}{3}m}n<34​m​, the chance that no two keys hash to the same slot is ≥23\geq\frac{2}{3}≥32​.

Proof Strategy:

  1. Compute the expected value of collisions. Note that collisions occurs when two different values are hashed to the same slot. (Indicator random variables)
  2. Apply a “tail” bound that converts the expected value to probability. (Markov’s inequality)
Compute the expected number of collisions

Let mmm be the size of the hash table. nnn is the number of keys in the set SSS. NNN is the size of the universe.

For inputs x,y∈S,x≠yx,y\in S,x\neq yx,y∈S,x=y, we define a random variable

Cxy={1if h(x)=h(y)0otherwiseC_{xy}= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h(x)=h(y) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}Cxy​={10​if h(x)=h(y)otherwise​

CxyC_{xy}Cxy​ is called an indicator random variable, that takes value 000 or 111.

The expected number of collisions is

E[Cxy]=1×Pr[Cxy=1]+0×Pr[Cxy=0]=Pr[Cxy=1]=1mE[C_{xy}]=1\times Pr[C_{xy}=1]+0\times Pr[C_{xy}=0]=Pr[C_{xy}=1]=\frac{1}{m}E[Cxy​]=1×Pr[Cxy​=1]+0×Pr[Cxy​=0]=Pr[Cxy​=1]=m1​

Define CxC_xCx​: random variable that represents the cost of inserting/searching/deleting xxx from the hash table.

Cx≤C_x\leqCx​≤ total number of elements that collide with xxx (= number of elements yyy such that h(x)=h(y)h(x)=h(y)h(x)=h(y)).

Cx=∑y∈S,y≠x,h(x)=h(y)1C_x=\sum_{y\in S,y\neq x,h(x)=h(y)}1Cx​=y∈S,y=x,h(x)=h(y)∑​1

So, Cx=∑y∈S,y≠xCxyC_x=\sum_{y\in S,y\neq x}C_{xy}Cx​=∑y∈S,y=x​Cxy​.

By linearity of expectation,

E[Cx]=∑y∈S,y≠xE[Cxy]=∑y∈S,y≠x1m=n−1mE[C_x]=\sum_{y\in S,y\neq x}E[C_{xy}]=\sum_{y\in S,y\neq x}\frac{1}{m}=\frac{n-1}{m}E[Cx​]=y∈S,y=x∑​E[Cxy​]=y∈S,y=x∑​m1​=mn−1​

E[C]=Θ(1)E[C]=\Theta(1)E[C]=Θ(1) if n=O(m)n=O(m)n=O(m). Total cost of KKK insert/search operations is O(k)O(k)O(k). by linearity of expectation.

Say CCC is the total number of collisions.

C=∑x∈SCx2C=\frac{\sum_{x\in S}C_x}{2}C=2∑x∈S​Cx​​ because each collision is counted twice.

E[C]=12∑x∈SE[Cx]=12∑x∈Sn−1m=n(n−1)2mE[C]=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{x\in S}E[C_x]=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{x\in S}\frac{n-1}{m}=\frac{n(n-1)}{2m}E[C]=21​x∈S∑​E[Cx​]=21​x∈S∑​mn−1​=2mn(n−1)​

If we want E[C]≤δE[C]\leq \deltaE[C]≤δ, then we need n=2mδn=\sqrt{2m\delta}n=2mδ​.

The probability of no collisions C=0C=0C=0

We know that the expected value of number of collisions is now ≤δ\leq \delta≤δ, but what about the probability of NO collisions?

Markov’s inequality: P[X≥k]≤E[X]kP[X\geq k]\leq\frac{E[X]}{k}P[X≥k]≤kE[X]​ For non-negative random variable XXX, Pr[X≥k⋅E[X]]≤1kPr[X\geq k\cdot E[X]]\leq \frac{1}{k}Pr[X≥k⋅E[X]]≤k1​.

Use Markov’s inequality: For non-negative random variable XXX, Pr[X≥k⋅E[X]]≤1kPr[X\geq k\cdot E[X]]\leq \frac{1}{k}Pr[X≥k⋅E[X]]≤k1​.

Apply this to CCC:

Pr[C≥1δE[C]]<δ⇒Pr[C≥1]<δPr[C\geq \frac{1}{\delta}E[C]]<\delta\Rightarrow Pr[C\geq 1]<\deltaPr[C≥δ1​E[C]]<δ⇒Pr[C≥1]<δ

So, if we want Pr[C=0]>1−δPr[C=0]>1-\deltaPr[C=0]>1−δ, n<2mδn<\sqrt{2m\delta}n<2mδ​ with probability 1−δ1-\delta1−δ, you will have no collisions.

More general conclusion

Claim: For a universal hash function family HHH, if number of keys n≤Bmδn\leq \sqrt{Bm\delta}n≤Bmδ​, then the probability that at most B+1B+1B+1 keys hash to the same slot is >1−δ> 1-\delta>1−δ.

Example: Quicksort

Based on partitioning [assume all elements are distinct]: Partition(A[p⋯r]A[p\cdots r]A[p⋯r])

  • Rearranges AAA into A[p⋯q−1],A[q],A[q+1⋯r]A[p\cdots q-1],A[q],A[q+1\cdots r]A[p⋯q−1],A[q],A[q+1⋯r]

Runtime: O(r−p)O(r-p)O(r−p), linear time.

def partition(A,p,r):
    x=A[r]
    lo=p
    for i in range(p,r):
        if A[i]<x:
            A[lo],A[i]=A[i],A[lo]
            lo+=1
    A[lo],A[r]=A[r],A[lo]
    return lo
 
def quicksort(A,p,r):
    if p<r:
        q=partition(A,p,r)
        quicksort(A,p,q-1)
        quicksort(A,q+1,r)

Runtime analysis

Let the number of element in AlowA_{low}Alow​ be kkk.

T(n)=Θ(n)+T(k)+T(n−k−1)T(n)=\Theta(n)+T(k)+T(n-k-1)T(n)=Θ(n)+T(k)+T(n−k−1)

By even split assumption, k=n2k=\frac{n}{2}k=2n​.

T(n)=T(n2)+T(n2−1)+Θ(n)≈Θ(nlog⁡n)T(n)=T(\frac{n}{2})+T(\frac{n}{2}-1)+\Theta(n)\approx \Theta(n\log n)T(n)=T(2n​)+T(2n​−1)+Θ(n)≈Θ(nlogn)

Which is approximately the same as merge sort.

Average case analysis is always suspicious.

Randomized Quicksort

  • Pick a random pivot element.
  • Analyze the expected runtime. over the random choices of pivot.
 
def randomized_partition(A,p,r):
    ix=random.randint(p,r)
    x=A[ix]
    A[r],A[ix]=A[ix],A[r]
    lo=p
    for i in range(p,r):
        if A[i]<x:
            A[lo],A[i]=A[i],A[lo]
            lo+=1
    A[lo],A[r]=A[r],A[lo]
    return lo
 
def randomized_quicksort(A,p,r):
    if p<r:
        q=randomized_partition(A,p,r)
        randomized_quicksort(A,p,q-1)
        randomized_quicksort(A,q+1,r)
E[T(n)]=E(T(n−k−1)+T(k)+cn)=E(T(n−k−1))+E(T(k))+cnE[T(n)]=E(T(n-k-1)+T(k)+cn)=E(T(n-k-1))+E(T(k))+cnE[T(n)]=E(T(n−k−1)+T(k)+cn)=E(T(n−k−1))+E(T(k))+cn

by linearity of expectation.

Pr[pivot has rank k]=1nPr[\textup{pivot has rank }k]=\frac{1}{n}Pr[pivot has rank k]=n1​

So,

E[T(n)]=1n∑k=0n−1(E[T(k)]+E[T(n−k−1)])+cn=cn+∑k=0n−1Pr[n−k−1=j]T(j)+∑k=0n−1Pr[k=j]T(j)=cn+∑k=0n−11nT(j)+∑k=0n−11nT(j)=cn+2n∑k=0n−1T(j)\begin{aligned} E[T(n)]&=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(E[T(k)]+E[T(n-k-1)])+cn\\ &=cn+\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}Pr[n-k-1=j]T(j)+\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}Pr[k=j]T(j)\\ &=cn+\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{1}{n}T(j)+\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{1}{n}T(j)\\ &=cn+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}T(j) \end{aligned}E[T(n)]​=n1​k=0∑n−1​(E[T(k)]+E[T(n−k−1)])+cn=cn+k=0∑n−1​Pr[n−k−1=j]T(j)+k=0∑n−1​Pr[k=j]T(j)=cn+k=0∑n−1​n1​T(j)+k=0∑n−1​n1​T(j)=cn+n2​k=0∑n−1​T(j)​

Claim: the solution to this recurrence is E[T(n)]=O(nlog⁡n)E[T(n)]=O(n\log n)E[T(n)]=O(nlogn) or T(n)=c′nlog⁡n+1T(n)=c'n\log n+1T(n)=c′nlogn+1.

Proof

We prove by induction.

Base case: n=1,T(n)=T(1)=cn=1,T(n)=T(1)=cn=1,T(n)=T(1)=c

Inductive step: Assume that T(k)=c′klog⁡k+1T(k)=c'k\log k+1T(k)=c′klogk+1 for all k<nk<nk<n.

Then,

T(n)=cn+2n∑k=0n−1T(k)=cn+2n∑k=0n−1(c′klog⁡k+1)=cn+2c′n∑k=0n−1klog⁡k+2n∑k=0n−11\begin{aligned} T(n)&=cn+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}T(k)\\ &=cn+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(c'k\log k+1)\\ &=cn+\frac{2c'}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}k\log k+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}1 \end{aligned}T(n)​=cn+n2​k=0∑n−1​T(k)=cn+n2​k=0∑n−1​(c′klogk+1)=cn+n2c′​k=0∑n−1​klogk+n2​k=0∑n−1​1​

Then we use the fact that ∑k=0n−1klog⁡k≤n2log⁡n2−n28\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}k\log k\leq \frac{n^2\log n}{2}-\frac{n^2}{8}∑k=0n−1​klogk≤2n2logn​−8n2​ (can be proved by induction).

T(n)=cn+2c′n(n2log⁡n2−n28)+2nn=c′nlog⁡n−14c′n+cn+2=(c′nlog⁡n+1)−(14c′n−cn−1)\begin{aligned} T(n)&=cn+\frac{2c'}{n}\left(\frac{n^2\log n}{2}-\frac{n^2}{8}\right)+\frac{2}{n}n\\ &=c'n\log n-\frac{1}{4}c'n+cn+2\\ &=(c'n\log n+1)-\left(\frac{1}{4}c'n-cn-1\right) \end{aligned}T(n)​=cn+n2c′​(2n2logn​−8n2​)+n2​n=c′nlogn−41​c′n+cn+2=(c′nlogn+1)−(41​c′n−cn−1)​

We need to prove that 14c′n−cn−1≥0\frac{1}{4}c'n-cn-1\geq 041​c′n−cn−1≥0.

Choose c′c'c′ and ccc such that 14c′n≥cn+1\frac{1}{4}c'n\geq cn+141​c′n≥cn+1 for all n≥2n\geq 2n≥2.

If c′≥8cc'\geq 8cc′≥8c, then T(n)≤c′nlog⁡n+1T(n)\leq c'n\log n+1T(n)≤c′nlogn+1.

E[T(n)]≤c′nlog⁡n+1=O(nlog⁡n)E[T(n)]\leq c'n\log n+1=O(n\log n)E[T(n)]≤c′nlogn+1=O(nlogn)

A more elegant proof:

Proof

Let XijX_{ij}Xij​ be an indicator random variable that is 111 if element of rank iii is compared to element of rank jjj.

Running time: X=∑i=0n−2∑j=i+1n−1XijX=\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1}X_{ij}X=∑i=0n−2​∑j=i+1n−1​Xij​

So, the expected number of comparisons is

E[Xij]=Pr[Xij=1]×1+Pr[Xij=0]×0=Pr[Xij=1]E[X_{ij}]=Pr[X_{ij}=1]\times 1+Pr[X_{ij}=0]\times 0=Pr[X_{ij}=1]E[Xij​]=Pr[Xij​=1]×1+Pr[Xij​=0]×0=Pr[Xij​=1]

This is equivalent to the expected number of comparisons in randomized quicksort.

The expected number of running time is

E[X]=E[∑i=0n−2∑j=i+1n−1Xij]=∑i=0n−2∑j=i+1n−1E[Xij]=∑i=0n−2∑j=i+1n−1Pr[Xij=1]\begin{aligned} E[X]&=E[\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1}X_{ij}]\\ &=\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1}E[X_{ij}]\\ &=\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1}Pr[X_{ij}=1] \end{aligned}E[X]​=E[i=0∑n−2​j=i+1∑n−1​Xij​]=i=0∑n−2​j=i+1∑n−1​E[Xij​]=i=0∑n−2​j=i+1∑n−1​Pr[Xij​=1]​

For any two elements zi,zj∈Sz_i,z_j\in Szi​,zj​∈S, the probability that ziz_izi​ is compared to zjz_jzj​ is (either ziz_izi​ or zjz_jzj​ is picked first as the pivot before the any elements of the ranks larger than iii and less than jjj)

Pr[Xij=1]=Pr[zi is picked first]+Pr[zj is picked first]=1j−i+1+1j−i+1=2j−i+1\begin{aligned} Pr[X_{ij}=1]&=Pr[z_i\text{ is picked first}]+Pr[z_j\text{ is picked first}]\\ &=\frac{1}{j-i+1}+\frac{1}{j-i+1}\\ &=\frac{2}{j-i+1} \end{aligned}Pr[Xij​=1]​=Pr[zi​ is picked first]+Pr[zj​ is picked first]=j−i+11​+j−i+11​=j−i+12​​

So, with harmonic number, Hn=∑k=1n1kH_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{k}Hn​=∑k=1n​k1​,

E[X]=∑i=0n−2∑j=i+1n−12j−i+1≤2∑i=0n−2∑k=1n−i−11k≤2∑i=0n−2clog⁡(n)=2clog⁡(n)∑i=0n−21=Θ(nlog⁡n)\begin{aligned} E[X]&=\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1}\frac{2}{j-i+1}\\ &\leq 2\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\sum_{k=1}^{n-i-1}\frac{1}{k}\\ &\leq 2\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}c\log(n)\\ &=2c\log(n)\sum_{i=0}^{n-2}1\\ &=\Theta(n\log n) \end{aligned}E[X]​=i=0∑n−2​j=i+1∑n−1​j−i+12​≤2i=0∑n−2​k=1∑n−i−1​k1​≤2i=0∑n−2​clog(n)=2clog(n)i=0∑n−2​1=Θ(nlogn)​
